title: Isaiah 33
subtitle: Woe to the Destroyer: The King in His Beauty

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “LORD, Be Gracious to Us; We Long for You”

heb.paragraph: "Woe to you, destroyer, you who have not been destroyed! When you stop destroying, you will be destroyed" (v.1). The sixth and final woe oracle. The destroyer (Assyria) will be destroyed by its own standard. The community prays: "LORD, be gracious to us (ḥānēnū); we long for you. Be our strength every morning, our salvation in time of distress" (v.2). "The LORD is exalted, for he dwells on high; he will fill Zion with his justice and righteousness. He will be the sure foundation for your times, a rich store of salvation and wisdom and knowledge; the fear of the LORD is the key to this treasure" (vv.5–6). The fear of the LORD as a key: unlocking salvation, wisdom, knowledge. The destroyer consumed by fire (vv.11–12).

notes.note: "Woe to you, destroyer! When you stop destroying, you will be destroyed." The boomerang principle: the method of destruction becomes the instrument of judgment.
"LORD, be gracious to us; we long for you. Be our strength every morning." Morning-by-morning dependence. Not stockpiled grace but daily provision.
"The fear of the LORD is the key to this treasure." Fear as key: not terror but reverence. The treasure: salvation, wisdom, knowledge. The fear of the LORD in Isaiah parallels Proverbs 1:7.

sections.header: Verses 13–24 — “Your Eyes Will See the King in His Beauty”

heb.paragraph: "Your eyes will see the King in his beauty (yōfî). They will see the land that stretches afar" (v.17). Who can dwell with the consuming fire? (v.14). Answer: "Those who walk righteously and speak what is right, who reject gain from extortion and keep their hands from accepting bribes, who stop their ears against plots of murder and shut their eyes against contemplating evil" (v.15). The entry requirements for God’s presence: ethical integrity, not ritual performance. Then the reward: "Your eyes will see the King in his beauty." The vision of God’s beauty is the culmination. Zion becomes a place of broad rivers (v.21). No illness (v.24). "The LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king; it is he who will save us" (v.22) — judge, lawgiver, king: the three branches of government, all held by God.

notes.note: "Who can dwell with the consuming fire? Those who walk righteously and speak what is right." The entry liturgy (cf. Ps 15; 24). Not how to enter the temple but how to survive God’s presence. The answer: moral integrity.
"Your eyes will see the King in his beauty." The beatific vision. The goal of all Isaiah’s prophecy: seeing God as he is. 1 John 3:2 echoes: "we shall see him as he is."

"The LORD is our judge, our lawgiver, our king; it is he who will save us." Three governmental roles: judicial, legislative, executive. All three = God. The most compact statement of theocracy in the OT.
"No one living in Zion will say, ‘I am ill.’" The final promise: no sickness. Revelation 21:4 develops this: no more death, mourning, crying, pain.

ppl.text: "The fear of the LORD is the key to this treasure…

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: " "The LORD is our judge, our lawgiver, our king…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe will be the sure foundation for your times, a rich store of salvation and wisdom and knowledge; the fear of the Lord is the key to this treasure.ESVand he will be the stability of your times, abundance of salvation, wisdom, and knowledge; the fear of the LORD is Zion’s treasure.NIVYour plunder, O nations, is harvested as by young locusts; like a swarm of locusts people pounce on it.ESVand your spoil is gathered as the caterpillar gathers; as locusts leap, it is leapt upon.

rec.text: ""Woe to you, destroyer! When you stop destroying, you will be destroyed." The boomerang principle: the method of destruction becomes the instrument of judgment."

rows.label: The Destroyer Destroyed — 33:1–12
rows.text: "The FEAR OF THE LORD is the KEY to this treasure." Morning-by-morning strength. The destroyer consumed.

hebtext.note: "Woe to you, destroyer, you who have not been destroyed! When you stop destroying, you will be destroyed" (v.1). The sixth and final woe oracle. The destroyer (Assyria) will be destroyed by its own standard. The community prays: "LORD, be gracious to us (ḥānēnū); we long for you. Be our strength every morning, our salvation in time of distress" (v.2). "The LORD is exalted, for he dwells on high; he will fill Zion with his justice and righteousness. He will be the sure foundation for your times, a rich store of salvation and wisdom and knowledge; the fear of the LORD is the key to this treasure" (vv.5–6). The fear of the LORD as a key: unlocking salvation, wisdom, knowledge. The destroyer consumed by fire (vv.11–12).

"Your eyes will see the King in his beauty (yōfî). They will see the land that stretches afar" (v.17). Who can dwell with the consuming fire? (v.14). Answer: "Those who walk righteously and speak what is right, who reject gain from extortion and keep their hands from accepting bribes, who stop their ears against plots of murder and shut their eyes against contemplating evil" (v.15). The entry requirements for God’s presence: ethical integrity, not ritual performance. Then the reward: "Your eyes will see the King in his beauty." The vision of God’s beauty is the culmination. Zion becomes a place of broad rivers (v.21). No illness (v.24). "The LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king; it is he who will save us" (v.22) — judge, lawgiver, king: the three branches of government, all held by God.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: judge, lawgiver, king — all three held by God. Worship and Holiness: the beatific vision, seeing the King in his beauty. The six woe oracles conclude with the ultimate reward: seeing God.
